State of California

Memorandum

To : Bob Blasier
     Bob Stern
     Ted Prim
     Tom Houston
     Barbara Milman

Date : June 1, 1982

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Judy Davis

Subject: GC-82/158
        Metropolitan Water District of Southern California (MWD)

Government expression is critical to the operation of
a democratic policy, but the power of governments to
communicate is also the power to destroy the under-
pinnings of government by consent. Yudof, "When
Governments Speak", 57 Tex. L.R. 867, 865 (1979).1/

* * * * *

A free marketplace of political ideas is essential to
the proper functioning of a democratic system. . . .
If a government can manipulate that marketplace, it
can ultimately subvert the processes by which the
people hold it accountable. It is just such
manipulation that the guarantee of a 'system of free-
dom of expression' was intended to prevent.
Kamenshive, "The First Amendment's Implied Political
Establishment Clause," 67 Cal. L.R. 1104, 1105-1106
(1979).

See also Shiffrin, "Government Speech" 27 U.C.L.A. L.R. (1980).

When respected commentators begin filing law review
articles at one another, the issue they address merits some con-
sideration. The broad constitutional issue addressed above
underlies the far more narrow one of whether MWD can be required
to report expenditures for a ballot measure. This memo attempts
to put MWD's activities relating to the Proposition 9/Peripheral
Canal (Prop. 9) ballot measure into their legal context by, with-
out exhaustive research, discussing our Act and regulations and
some of the cases and commentaries which address the issues raised
so that this agency can adopt a position with awareness of the
legal as well as practical ramifications.

1/ As this is an informal memo, cites are casual throughout.

GC-82/158
June 1, 1982
Page 2

     The Commission's present position, as reflected in its
regulations, is that government agencies which make expenditures
or contributions must file campaign statements if they qualify
as a committee under Government Code Section 82013.  2 Cal Adm.
Code Section 18420.  The comment to that regulation notes that it
is not to be construed as "condoning or authorizing campaign-
related activities" by governmental agencies.

     Contributions include non-monetary payments made at the
behest (under the control or at the direction) of organizations,
such as Citizens, formed for the purpose of attempting to influ-
ence the actions of the voters for passage of a measure such as
Proposition 9.  2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215.  However, there is
scant evidence in the public record of MWD activities from which
to conclude that MWD qualifies as a major donor committee.  After
the Canal issue was certified, few MWD documents refer to inter-
action with Citizens and/or its consultant, Braun Campaigns, Inc.
From the records, it is not clear whether MWD assistant manager
Kennedy ever met with Citizens to "determine how MWD could assist
their efforts," nor is it clear that the agency's contact with Braun
Campaigns involvement of MWD's activities with Citizens'.  The
"special tour" arranged for Braun personnel, if a contribution,
does not appear to have cost over $5,000 in MWD funds.  As we have
determined not to investigate further, we must conclude that no
reportable contributions were made.

     It is clear that MWD has expended money for "communications"
in which a "clearly identified" - by "proposition number, official
title, or popular name" - ballot measure was referenced; however,
no"express words of advocacy" were used.  Whether those communica-
tions "otherwise refer" to the ballot measure so that "taken as a
whole," they "unambiguously urge" passage of Proposition 9 is dis-
puted general consensus seems to be that, as a matter of fact,
they do not.  2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18225.

     In reaching that consensus, the communications were judged
by appraisal of their content alone, with reference to no
extraneous factors, an approach believed to be dictated by Buckley
v. Valeo, 96 S. Ct. 612, at 646-647 and 663-664, and FEC v.
C.L.I.T.R.I.M., 616 P2d 45 at 53.  Various practical factors, such
as the difficulty of making the factual determination that a
communication was indeed an expenditure, were also considered.
Following our last meeting, my impression was that the Commission's
position was to be that with respect to expenditures made by public
agencies, the "otherwise refer" standard of 18225 would simply not
be applied to ballot measure campaigns; expenditures by governmen-
tal agencies would be reportable only if express words of advocacy
were used.  The impression left with at least one other partici-
pant in the meeting was that even if express words of advocacy

GC-82/158
June 1, 1982
Page 3

were employed, we would not require government agencies to report
expenditures regarding ballot measures. Before we take either of
these positions, and however we choose to do so, an examination of
cases in which both the legal and practical considerations were
addressed, at least tangentially, might be helpful.

First, it is clear that were we concerned here with in-kind
contributions from a public agency to a candidate, our position would
be that such contributions are reportable. In FPPC v. Suitt, 90 CA3
125 (1979), we strongly argued that the practical difficulties in
distinguishing between campaign activity and legislative activity
should in no way be determinative: "The FPPC points out that
analogous line-drawing is required and has taken place in other
types of campaign activity." 90 CA3 at 131. The court in Suitt
analogized to Stanson v. Mott, 17 C3 206 (1979), "The Legislature's
asserted difficulty here, if indeed it exists, is no different than
that faced by government officials in distinguishing between
improper expenditures of public funds for 'campaign' purposes and
the proper expenditure thereof for 'informational' activities."
90 CA3 at 130. Pointing out that the contributions involved were
illegal, the court observed:

     It does not follow however that such expenditures were
     meant to be unreportable, for the electorate would
     then be saying in effect: "We recognize that such a use
     of public funds is illegal and unconstitutional, but,
     where it nonetheless occurs, it must be kept secret."
     This is absurd.

The act undeniably was intended to deal comprehensively with the influence of money, all money, on
electoral and governmental processes. Id. at 131.

     Although the alleged activities in Suitt were "unambiguously
political," Id. at 131, the case is instructive because it puts
us on record as not shying away from difficult line-drawing in an
area which is as much the Attorney General's province as are expenditures regarding ballot measures, for both "contributions" to
candidates and "expenditures" on behalf of ballot measures by
public agencies involve illegal use of public funds. Stanson, supra.
Where public agency expenditures are for communications relating to
ballot measures, which do contain express words of advocacy, not to
require them to be reported where the agency has otherwise qualified
as an independent expenditure committee would be, in my opinion, to
retreat from the victory won in Suitt. Certainly such a position
would not result in disclosure of ". . . all money . . ." expended
on the ballot measure campaign.

GC-82/158
June 1, 1982
Page 4

     Important distinctions, however, have been drawn between
contributions and expenditures, and between candidate and ballot
measures. It is by no means certain that those distinctions justify
our taking a different position with respect to expenditures for
ballot measures as opposed to contributions to candidates when it is
a public agency that is involved in the political process.

     One case making these distinctions is First National Bank of
Boston v. Bellotti, 98 S.Ct. 1407 (1978). The case is significant
for several reasons, the most important of which is that the
Supreme Court has been afforded an opportunity to extend Bellotti
to municipal corporations and has declined to do so. To enable you
to appreciate the importance of that refusal, Bellotti's holding,
the reasoning for it, and the alignment of the Court in rendering
the decision will be reviewed in some detail.

     In Bellotti, the Supreme Court invalidated on First Amendment
grounds that portion of a Massachusetts law which prohibited
certain profit-making corporations, such as the bank, from making
expenditures to oppose a ballot measure concerning individual
taxation, reversing the holding of the Massachusetts Supreme Court
that the First Amendment rights of a corporation are limited to
issues that materially affect its business, property, or assets,
expenditures for which were permissible under the law being
challenged. Specifically, Bellotti held that:

     Because that portion of (the Massachusetts law)
     challenged by appellants prohibits protected speech in
     a manner unjustified by a compelling state interest, it
     must be invalidated. Id. at 1426.

     In reaching its decision, the Court found first that the kind
of speech the bank intended to engage in - "appellate wanted to spend
money to publicize their views on a proposed constitutional amendment
that was to be submitted to the voters as a ballot question at a
general election . . ." Id. at 1412 - was protected by First
Amendment guarantees, for ". . . 'there is practically universal
agreement that a major purpose of (the First) Amendment was to pro-
tect the free discussion of governmental affairs.'" Id. at 1416.
Second, the Court found that the corporation's right to speak freely
was guaranteed it by the liberty clause of the Fourteenth Amendment,
and that therefore the Massachusetts legislature

GC-82/158
June 1, 1982
Page 5


. . . is constitutionally disqualified from dictating
the subjects about which persons may speak and the
speakers who may address a public issue.(cites) If a
legislature may direct business corporations to 'stick
to business', it may also limit other corporations -
religious, charitable, or civic - to their respective
'business' when addressing the public. Such power of
government to channel the expression of views is
unacceptable under the First Amendment. Id. at 1420.

Third, the Court found that Massachusetts had no compelling state
interest in prohibiting corporate speech on ballot measures dealing
with individual taxation or otherwise unrelated to the corporation's
business. The court stated that if the state's argument were
supported by evidence that ". . . corporate advocacy threatened
imminently to undermine democratic processes . . .", those argu-
ments ". . . would merit our consideration . . . Without such
evidence, the state's allegations of compelling interest were not
. . . inherently persuasive . . . ", for "[r]efera are held on
issues, not candidates for public office. The risk of corruption
perceived in cases involving candidate elections . . . simply is
not present in a popular vote on a public issue." Id. at 1423.

Bellotti was a 5-4 decision, Stewart, Blackmun and Stevens
joining Powell in the Court's opinion, with which Chief Justice
Burger separately concurred. White, joined by Brennan and Marshall,
dissented, arguing that state restriction on political activity of
business corporations, is a means of

. . . preventing institutions which have been permitted
to amass wealth as a result of special advantages ex-
tended by the State for certain economic purposes from
using that wealth to acquire an unfair advantage in the
political process . . . The State need not permit its
own creation to consume it. Id. at 1433.

Rehnquist dissented separately, arguing that the liberty clause of
the Fourteenth Amendment protects ". . . natural, not artificial
persons . . ." Id. at 1439, to which rule he observed that the
court had previously made only narrow exceptions.

In the aftermath of Bellotti, the City of Boston (a municipal
corporation) determined that it wished to make expenditures to
support a measure on the November 1978 Massachusetts ballot. It
appropriated close to $1,000,000 for the campaign, set up a separate
office to handle it, and entered into contracts with consultants
(one of which was a $100,000 contract with Butcher-Forde). The city
intended not only to disseminate "information" but to expressly
urge passage of the measure.

GC-82/158
June 1, 1982
Page 6

Suit was filed seeking to enjoin the proposed expenditures
by the City of Boston. The Massachusetts Supreme Court granted
the injunction, Anderson v. City of Boston, 380 NE2 628 (Mass.
1978); opinion attached. The City then applied to Justice Brennan
for a stay of the judgment enjoining them from ". . . expending
city funds in support of a referendum proposal on the ballot of
the November 1978 general election." City of Boston v. Anderson,
99 S. Ct. 50 (10/20/78).

Brennan granted the stay, describing the holding of the
Massachusetts court as being that even if

    . . . 'constitutionally protected speech includes the
right of a municipality to speak militantly about a
referendum issue . . . where the legislature . . . has
said it may not,' . . . there are demonstrated, compelling interests of the Commonwealth which justify the
'restraints' which the Commonwealth has placed on the
city . . . ', namely, '(t)he Commonwealth has an
interest in assuring that a dissenting minority of taxpayers is not compelled to finance the expression on an
election of views with which they disagree.' Id.

Finding that the City would suffer the greater harm if the injunction were not stayed, and that it was likely that at least four
members of the Supreme Court would vote to grant a writ of certiorari,
Brennan gave Boston the green light. In balancing the equities,
Brennan reasoned that since Bellotti had left corporate opponents
free to finance their opposition, the City - if the stay were not
granted - would be ". . . forever denied any opportunity to finance
communications to the . . . electorate of its views in support of
the referendum . . ." Id.

Within days of the election, the full court heard a motion to
vacate the stay. Anderson v. City of Boston, 99 S. Ct. 346 (Nov.
6, 1978). The motion was denied. Rehnquist, joined by Stevens and
Stewart (who had both been with the majority in Bellotti) dissented.
He pointed out that Massachusetts had held that Boston "'. . .
has no authority to appropriate funds . . . to influence the result
of a referendum proposed to be submitted to the people at a State
election.'" He reasoned:

    Unless state action has violated some federal law, a
federal court has no power to compel a State to spend its
money or to grant a political subdivision of the State
authority which the State has withheld. Federal questions may . . . arise when a State regulates the communicative activities of third parties whether they be
individuals or private corporations. Such questions may
also arise if a State authorizes expenditures to advocate

GC-82/158
June 1, 1982
Page 7

or explain. But in this case we are merely confronted
with 'a state's determination to refrain from speech
on a given topic . . . and to bar its various sub-
divisions from expending funds in contravention of
that determination.' I consider it frivolous to
suggest that the First Amendment, or any other provi-
sion of the United States Constitution, empowers this
court to interfere with that determination. (all
emphasis added)

In January of 1979, the Supreme Court dismissed the appeal
from the decision of the Massachusetts Court enjoining the
expenditures . . . for want of a substantial federal question."
City of Boston v. Anderson, 99 S. Ct. 822 (Jan. 1, 1979). Brennan,
Blackmun, and Powell would have noted probable jurisdiction.

Dismissing the appeal had the effect of letting stand the
Massachusetts court's now useless injunction (Boston spent the
money, and the referendum issue passed). The dismissal was also
a judgment on the merits. In my opinion, there is no way the
court could have rendered that judgment without essentially accept-
ing Rehnquist's reasoning. Professor Tribe, representing Boston,
had argued strenuously that Bellotti should be extended to cover
municipal corporations. His argument did not prevail. Yudof,
supra, 57 Tex. L.R. 863 at 866.

So we appear to be left with a decision that says, in effect,
that a state may restrict the participation of government agencies
in ballot measure campaigns without raising any substantial ques-
tions of federal constitutional law. It is certainly arguable that
given that decision, the sweeping language in Bellotti - that states
may never restrict constitutionally protected speech based on "the
identity of the speaker" - does not apply when the speaker is the
government itself.

It is likewise arguable that the language in Buckley and
C.L.I.T.R.I.M., supra, that " . . . the words 'expressly advocating'
means (sic) exactly what they say," 616 F2 at 53, is not applicable
where the speaker doing the advocating is a public agency, which,
according to City of Boston, has no constitutional right to engage
in political debate where the legislature has said it may not.

The standards enunciated in Stanson clearly envisage that
communications such as MWD's which contain no words of express ad-
vocacy may nevertheless be proscribed:

GC-82/158
June 1, 1982
Page 8


     . . . (I)n a number of instances policy financed
brochures . . . which have purported to contain only
relevant factual information, and which have refrained
from exhorting voters to "Vote Yes," have nevertheless
been found to constitute improper campaign literature
(cities).  In such cases, the determination of the pro-
priety or impropriety of the expenditures depends on a
careful consideration of such factors as the style,
tenor and timing of the publication; no hard and fast
rule governs every case.  17 C3 at 222 (see also
F.N. 8)

In promulgating these standards - virtually identical to the
"otherwise see-saw" and "taken as a whole" language of our regulation -
the Stanson court made no reference to the "expressly advocate"
language of Buckley.  Like Rehnquist in City of Boston, the Stanson
court saw the "serious constitutional question" arising not from
legislative restrictions on public agency expenditures for ballot
measures, but from " . . . explicit legislative authorization of
the use of public funds for partisan campaigning."  17 C3 at 219
(emphasis added).

     Where personal liberties are involved, there is no doubt but
that Buckley controls.  Buckley held that the reporting of indepen-
dent expenditures for the purpose of "influencing" an election
could not be constitutionally required unless expenditures were
construed to mean " . . . only funds used for communications that
expressly advocate . . . ."  To interpret "influencing" less restric-
tively would result in impermissible overbreadth and a consequent
denial of due process to those who faced potential criminal liability
for failing to report such expenditures.  That these due process
considerations should be determinative when the expenditures are of
public funds is questionable.  Any public official who " . . . uses
(public moneys) for a purpose not authorized by law . . . is
punishable by imprisonment in the state prison for two, three, or
four years, and is disqualified from holding office in this state."
Penal Code Section 424(2).  Vagueness and overbreadth challenges to
this provision were rejected in People v. Battin, 77 CA3 635 (1978),
cert. den. 99 S.Ct. 183, in which the court cited to Stanson.

     The grave constitutional questions arising from government
attempts to influence election results make expenditures by govern-
ment agencies "particularly suspect," for these activities occur
"at a critical point in the democratic process."  Yudof, supra,
at 916.  All of us agree that "[t]he line between political and
nonpolitical speech" by government agencies " . . . may be difficult
to draw in some instances," but that difficulty " . . . does not

GC-82/158
June 1, 1982
Page 9

undercut its validity." Kamenshire, supra, at 1113. We have expressed our determination to draw those lines in other areas of political activity - witness Suitt and the Brown investigation. The fact of the matter is that public agencies may make in-kind contributions to candidates, but when it comes to ballot measures, they will be far more likely to make expenditures. The issues posed by MWD will be before us again. I would urge that we take the position that where those expenditures are for communications which do expressly advocate, we require them to be reported. Where the communications contain no express words of advocacy, I believe the Stanson standards are applicable. We gave no consideration to factors such as "style, tenor and timing" in our evaluation of MWD's materials. Not to do so is to back away from drawing lines in an area of fundamental constitutional import.

The precise question posed by MWD's activities has, of course, not been addressed by any court. Boston's expenditures were for communications which did contain express words of advocacy. The expenditures in C.L.I.P.R.I.M. were by a private organization, contained no express words of advocacy, made reference to no particular election, and were made with respect to a candidate, as opposed to a ballot measure. So the issue of whether the government itself can constitutionally be required to report expenditures which "otherwise refer" to a ballot measure remains undecided. Underlying the determination that public agencies can or cannot be required to report these types of expenditures is the issue of the government's role in the election process. Any position we take on the MWD matter needs to be evaluated in the light of what we consider that role properly to be.

est by excluding its political subdivisions
from partisan involvement in election ques-

Its stated purposes are "to provide for pub-
lic disclosure of political contributions and
expenditures, and the regulation of said
contributions and expenditures." See St.
1975, c. 151. "Expenditure" is defined to
include an expenditure by a political committee "for the purpose of promoting or
opposing a . . . referendum question
. . . or other question submitted to the
voters." § 1. That chapter regulates political committees which are defined to include "any committee, association, organization or other group of persons,
which makes expenditures
for the purpose of opposing or promoting
a referendum question
. . . or other question submitted to the voters."
Id. It regulates the expenditure of money
by such a committee (G.L. c. 55, § 5, 8),
and sets forth detailed reporting requirements (G.L. c. 55, § 18). Chapter 55 also
places restrictions on the expenditure of
funds by corporations, making explicit reference to various kinds of corporations, although not to a municipal corporation.
§ 8.¹

We interpret G.L. c. 55 as intended to
reach at political fund raising and expenditures within the Commonwealth. The absence of any reference to municipal corporations is significant, or as an indication
that municipal action to influence election
results was intended to be exempt from
regulation, but rather as an indication that
the Legislature did not even contemplate
such municipal action could occur. We note finally that traditionally municipalities

ties have not appropriated funds to influence election results. If the Legislature
had expected that municipalities would engage in such activities or intended that they
could, G.L. c. 55 would have regulated those
activities as well. We thus construe G.L. c.
55 as preempting any right which a municipality might otherwise have to appropriate
funds for the purpose of influencing the
result on a referendum question to be submitted to the people at a State election.

Our conclusion that municipalities are
barred from political activities of the character involved is strengthened by other
provisions of G.L. c. 55. Section 13 forbids
a municipal employee (among others), other
than an elected officer, from soliciting or
receiving any payment of money for any
political purpose whatsoever. No person shall solicit or receive any payment of money for
such a purpose in a building occupied for
municipal purposes. G.L. c. 55, § 14. Generally, no person in the service of a city
shall deliver to another such person any
money to be applied to the promotion of
any political object whatever. G.L. c. 55,
§ 15, as appearing in St.1975, c. 151, § 1.
Literally, these prohibitions apply to moneys
appropriated by a municipality as to any
they do to funds derived from any other
source. Even if they are ignored should
not be carried as acting municipal appropriation. They demonstrate a general
legislative intent to keep political raising and disbursing out of the hands of
nonelective public employees and out of any
city or town halls.²

embedding.qa_text: **Memorandum** To: Bob Blasier Bob Stern Ted Prim Tom Houston Barbara Milman Date: June 1, 1982 From: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION Judy Davis Subject: GC-82/158 Metropolitan Water District of Southern California (MWD) Government expression is critical to the operation of a democratic polity, but the power of governments to communicate is also the power to destroy the underpinnings of government by consent. Yudof, "When Governments Speak", 57 Tex. L.R. 863, 865 (1979).1/ * * * * * A free marketplace of political ideas is essential to the proper functioning of a democratic system. . . . If a government can manipulate that marketplace, it can ultimately subvert the processes by which the people hold it accountable. It is just such manipulation that the guarantee of a 'system of freedom of expression' was intended to prevent. Kamenshire, "The First Amendment's Implied Political Establishment Clause," 67 Cal. L.R. 1104, 1105-1106 (1979). See also Shiffrin, "Government Speech" 27 U.C.L.A. L.R. (1980). When respected commentators begin flinging law review articles at one another, the issue they address merits some consideration. The broad constitutional issue addressed above underlies the far more narrow one of whether MWD can be required to report expenditures for a ballot measure. This memo attempts to put MWD's activities relating to the Proposition 9/Peripheral Canal (Prop. 9) ballot measure into their legal context by, without exhaustive research, discussing our Act and regulations and some of the cases and commentaries which address the issues raised so that this agency can adopt a position with awareness of the legal as well as practical ramifications. 1/ As this is an informal memo, cites are casual throughout. The Commission's present position, as reflected in its regulations, is that government agencies which make expenditures or contributions must file campaign statements if they qualify as a committee under Government Code Section 82013. 2 Cal Adm. Code Section 18420. The comment to that regulation notes that it is not to be construed as "condoning or authorizing campaign-related activities" by governmental agencies. Contributions include non-monetary payments made at the behest (under the control or at the direction) of organizations, such as Citizens, formed for the purpose of attempting to influence the actions of the voters for passage of a measure such as Proposition 9. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215. However, there is scant evidence in the public record of MWD activities from which to conclude that MWD qualifies as a major donor committee. After the Canal issue was certified, few MWD documents refer to interaction with Citizens and/or its consultant, Braun Campaigns, Inc. From the records, it is not clear whether MWD assistant manager Kennedy ever met with Citizens to "determine how MWD could assist their efforts," nor is it clear that the agency's contact with Braun campaigns involved coordination of MWD's activities with Citizens'. The "special tour" arranged for Braun personnel, if a contribution, does not appear to have cost over $5,000 in MWD funds. As we have determined not to investigate further, we must conclude that
